title: Open Source Low Tech
author: grep_it
relevanceReason: 命中 1 个 AI/科技关键词。
summaryRaw: My name is Daniel Connell. I prototype and develop basic technologies which anyone can make using recycled materials and simple tools. The aim is for everyone everywhere to be able to build and maintain their own infrastructure; producing their own energy, food, clean water, communications, and anything else they need. All designs are open source and license free for any purpose, and full construction tutorials and how-tos are available here. The Facebook group is also a good place to ask questions and post results from your own builds. Featured In: Al Jazeera ¦ The Guardian ¦ New Statesman ¦ Le Monde ¦ Makezine

comments.author: WillAdams
comments.textRawHtml:
Ages ago, I had Vol. 2, _The Metal Lathe_ which was one of the 7 vol. "Gingery Books", a series on _Build Your Own Metal Working Shop from Scrap_ (Vol. 1 was _The Charcoal Foundry_) which used as its central conceit the fact that a lathe is the one tool in a machine shop suited to easily replicating itself (insert old machinist joke about how you can make anything with a shaper, except money).

Always regretted giving my original away, the typewritten text and hand-drawings had a certain charm which the updated single leatherbound hardcover lack.

The Gingery Book Store is shutting down this year, and not reprinting any books, so one wonders what will replace it:

https://forum.makerforums.info/t/google-post-by-marcus-wolsc...

Another effort along these lines was the "Multimachine" which attempted to create a metal-working equivalent to the woodworking shop's ShopSmith using an engine block, taking advantage of the fact that they are readily and inexpensively available from junked vehicles. I believe it was on the "Opensource Ecology" site linked elsethread.

Perhaps Chris Borge's series of machine tools made with 3D printed shells filled with concrete and arrayed with hardware store components?

https://hackaday.com/2025/04/13/3d-printed-milling-machine-i...

That said, as various machinists have joked, I am still "shaper curious", and have been sketching up a hand-operated shaper --- debating on saving for a set of castings, making my own, or going the hardware store route....
comments.textZhHtml:
很久以前，我有卷。 2、《金属车床》7卷之一。 “Gingery Books”是一个关于_用废品建立你自己的金属加工车间_的系列（第一卷是_木炭铸造厂_），它的中心思想是车床是机械车间中唯一适合轻松复制自身的工具（插入机械师的老笑话，关于如何用成型机制造任何东西，除了钱）。

一直后悔把我的原作送人，打字的文字和手绘有一定的魅力，这是更新的单本皮装精装所缺乏的。

Gingery 书店今年将关闭，并且不再重印任何书籍，因此人们想知道什么会取代它：

https://forum.makerforums.info/t/google-post-by-marcus-wolsc...

沿着这条线的另一项努力是“Multimachine”，它试图利用发动机组制造出与木工车间的 ShopSmith 相当的金属加工设备，利用了可以从报废车辆中轻松且廉价地获得发动机组的优势。我相信它位于链接 elsethread 的“开源生态”网站上。

也许 Chris Borge 的系列机床是用 3D 打印的外壳制成的，外壳填充了混凝土，并排列着五金店的组件？

https://hackaday.com/2025/04/13/3d-printed-milling-machine-i...

也就是说，正如许多机械师开玩笑的那样，我仍然“对造型师感到好奇”，并且一直在草拟一个手动操作造型师的草图——争论是攒钱买一套铸件，自己做，还是走五金店路线……

comments.author: Duanemclemore
comments.textRawHtml:
Victor Papanek's Design for the Real World [0] is a foundational text in the area of Appropriate Technology (cited here by others).

Essential reading alongside the Illich (also linked by others), Pye's Nature and Art of Workmanship [1], and Winner's "Do Artifacts Have Politics?" [2] for those interested in.... Let's call it an ecology OF technology?

And also the Papanek was probably the book that felt most like a forbidden text in undergraduate architecture school...

[0] https://archive.org/details/designforrealwor0000papa/page/n6...

[1] https://archive.org/details/natureartofwor00pyed

[2] https://www.jstor.org/stable/20024652
comments.textZhHtml:
Victor Papanek 的《真实世界设计》[0] 是适当技术领域的基础文本（在此被其他人引用）。

与伊里奇（Illich）（也由其他人链接）、派伊（Pye）的《自然与工艺艺术》（Nature and Art of Workmanship）[1]以及温纳（Winner）的“手工艺品有政治吗？”一起阅读[2] 对于那些对...感兴趣的人，我们可以称之为技术生态吗？

而且《帕帕内克》可能是建筑学院本科生中最感觉违禁的一本书……

[0] https://archive.org/details/designforrealwor0000papa/page/n6...

[1] https://archive.org/details/natureartofwor00pyed

[2] https://www.jstor.org/stable/20024652

comments.author: xmprt
comments.textRawHtml:
I like the idea behind this. I feel like far too often, the solutions we build for poor communities involve specific materials that can't be manufactured locally, so it just creates more dependence rather than self-sufficiency.

It's one thing to build and ship 1000 bicycles to a poor village, but it's another to teach a village how to make bicycles with random spare pipes and materials they can find anywhere. That way if something breaks, they have the skillset to fix it.

If you go to villages in developing nations, you'll see these kinds of innovative solutions all over - things that don't seem like they should work but they just do after lots of trial and error.
comments.textZhHtml:
我喜欢这背后的想法。我觉得我们为贫困社区构建的解决方案经常涉及无法在当地制造的特定材料，因此它只会造成更多的依赖而不是自给自足。

制造 1000 辆自行车并将其运送到一个贫穷的村庄是一回事，但教一个村庄如何使用随处可见的备用管道和材料来制造自行车则是另一回事。这样，如果出现问题，他们就有能力修复它。

如果你去发展中国家的村庄，你会看到到处都有这种创新的解决方案——这些东西看起来不应该起作用，但经过大量的试验和错误后它们就会起作用。

children.author: nchmy
children.textRawHtml:
I strongly agree that it's incomparably more important to teach a man/village how to fish/build a bike than to give them one. Unfortunately most people who focus on "helping" are grossly incompetent and have largely misaligned incentives (and oversight).

As for local innovation, it think it very much depends on where. I've visited and lived in many communities in developing nations in Latin America and there's a distinct dearth of not just innovative solutions, but even just basic and seemingly obvious ones. Upon seeing and feeling this 7 years ago, I decided to dedicate my life to it. I'm hopeful that in the coming year I'll finally be ready to share what I've been working on to facilitate it in a more scalable way...
children.textZhHtml:
我强烈同意，教一个人/一个村庄如何钓鱼/制造一辆自行车比给他们一辆重要得多。不幸的是，大多数专注于“帮助”的人都非常无能，而且激励（和监督）很大程度上失调。

至于本地创新，它认为这很大程度上取决于哪里。我访问过并居住在拉丁美洲发展中国家的许多社区，那里不仅明显缺乏创新的解决方案，而且甚至缺乏基本的和看似显而易见的解决方案。七年前，当我看到并感受到这一点时，我决定为此奉献一生。我希望在来年我终于准备好分享我一直在努力以更可扩展的方式促进它的工作......

children.author: jvanderbot
children.textRawHtml:
When I went to South Africa last, we went through a few slums.

It was comical to see the celebrity "assistance" in the form of e.g., a music production studio + school set up by skrillex, neighboring areas where people lived in shipping crates with stolen electricity jumpered directly from main, and with water tapped off city lines and bucketted in.

They dont need djs, they need plubmbers, carpenters, and electricians first! Fires and water loss were endemic. Almost everyone lived off government assistance, stole / smuggled resources, and blocked traffic/protested when the illegal resource taps were shut down or a government job didn't manifest. The people who made money where they lived usually washed cars or sold trinkets to tourists or brewed bucket beer.
children.textZhHtml:
我上次去南非时，我们经过了一些贫民窟。

看到名人以例如 skrillex 建立的音乐制作工作室和学校的形式提供“援助”是很滑稽的，邻近地区的人们住在船运箱里，偷来的电直接从主电源跳线，水从城市线路中抽出并装在桶里。

他们不需要DJ，他们首先需要水管工、木匠和电工！火灾和水流失十分普遍。几乎每个人都靠政府援助为生，偷窃/走私资源，并在非法资源水龙头被关闭或政府工作没有体现时阻塞交通/抗议。在他们居住的地方赚钱的人通常会洗车或向游客出售小饰品或酿造桶装啤酒。

children.author: nephihaha
children.textRawHtml:
Music, much like sport, is seen by many working class people as a way to make some money, so not completely senseless. Art to a lesser extent.

Carpenters/joiners are a good half-way house, as they can provide both practical assistance to the community as well as selling collectibles/souvenirs or whatever.

The problem is the minute some of these people pick up skills or money, they leave the slums behind. The plumbers and electricians may make better money rigging up office blocks than from fitting out the slums.
children.textZhHtml:
音乐，就像体育运动一样，被许多工人阶级视为赚钱的一种方式，所以并不是完全没有意义。艺术程度较小。

木匠/细木工是一个很好的中途之家，因为他们既可以为社区提供实际帮助，也可以出售收藏品/纪念品或其他东西。

问题是，一旦这些人中的一些人获得了技能或金钱，他们就离开了贫民窟。水管工和电工安装办公大楼可能比装修贫民窟赚更多的钱。

children.author: mschuster91
children.textRawHtml:
> I strongly agree that it's incomparably more important to teach a man/village how to fish/build a bike than to give them one. Unfortunately most people who focus on "helping" are grossly incompetent and have largely misaligned incentives (and oversight).

It goes deeper than that if you ask me. It's rarely about "helping" in a lot of cases in the first place, and there's no incompetence at play either.

Just look at food aid to Africa. You know, the campaigns with banners of emaciated African children holding empty food bowls. These aren't primarily about helping African children, but about diverting European (or American) oversupply towards Africa to stabilize prices in the domestic markets - and they all but wiped out African food industry. Simbabwe was known as the "corn chamber of Africa" but lost that in a matter of decades as the cheap food from Europe was cheaper than they could produce. Or clothing donations, these ended up as "mitumba" in Africa, and wrecked local supply chains so hard that, by the time the Chinese came around, there was nothing left to fight.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我强烈同意，教一个人/一个村庄如何钓鱼/制造一辆自行车比给他们一辆重要得多。不幸的是，大多数专注于“帮助”的人都非常无能，而且激励（和监督）很大程度上失调。

如果你问我的话，事情会比这更深入。在很多情况下，一开始就很少涉及“帮助”，而且也不存在无能的情况。

只要看看对非洲的粮食援助就知道了。你知道，这些活动的横幅上都是瘦弱的非洲儿童，手里拿着空食物碗。这些主要不是为了帮助非洲儿童，而是为了将欧洲（或美国）的过剩供应转移到非洲，以稳定国内市场的价格——它们几乎摧毁了非洲的食品工业。辛巴布韦被称为“非洲玉米商会”，但在几十年内就失去了这个称号，因为来自欧洲的廉价食品比他们生产的便宜。或者衣物捐赠，这些最终在非洲被称为“mitumba”，严重破坏了当地的供应链，以至于当中国人回过神来时，已经没有什么可以抗争的了。

children.author: debo_
children.textRawHtml: The White Man's Burden by William Easterly covered this in some detail quite a few years ago.
children.textZhHtml: 威廉·伊斯特利（William Easterly）的《白人的负担》（White Man's Burden）几年前就详细介绍了这一点。

children.author: nephihaha
children.textRawHtml:
Apart from the obvious disclaimer that some parts of Africa function much better than others...

Zimbabwe's agricultural sector was wrecked by handing farms to Mugabe loyalists who didn't know what to do with them. His government didn't just go for whites, but also some of the black farm workers who would have known how to use them.

Likewise, the famine in Ethiopia was not helped by either Mengistu's Marxist government (who used Live Aid money for arms) or the previous set up under Emperor Haile Selassie which was based on clientism.
children.textZhHtml:
除了明显的免责声明外，非洲某些地区的功能比其他地区好得多……

津巴布韦的农业部门因将农场交给穆加贝的忠诚者而遭到破坏，而穆加贝的忠诚者不知道如何处理这些农场。他的政府不仅支持白人，还支持一些懂得如何利用白人的黑人农场工人。

同样，埃塞俄比亚的饥荒也没有得到门格斯图领导的马克思主义政府（他们用现场援助资金购买武器）和海尔·塞拉西皇帝建立的依附主义政府的帮助。

children.author: mschuster91
children.textRawHtml: Yeah, a bunch of Africa's problems were entirely self-inflicted. But... Western nations just dumping their surplus on Africa under the guise of "donations" certainly didn't help.
children.textZhHtml: 是的，非洲的许多问题完全是自己造成的。但是……西方国家只是打着“捐赠”的幌子将其盈余倾销给非洲，这肯定无济于事。

children.author: nchmy
children.textRawHtml:
In my fairly extensive experience, non-profit organizations are not just full of and run by grossly incompetent people, but deeply arrogant and/or deluded ones as well. I have NEVER found an organization that has a genuine desire to seek truth, efficacy etc. They often go with whatever their first (inevitably insufficient) idea was, and not only reject all criticism but respond with indignity, etc...

There's no meaningful/competent oversight. It's all just about feels and optics. And thus no real progress has or will be made.

Anyway, yes, I agree that competent and genuine people (who are extremely rare) ought to try to make a meaningful impact in the world. But there's generally more money in something else.

(one rare exception that comes to mind, though i haven't visited them, is The Ocean Cleanup project. They seem to be experimenting and succeeding towards the worthwhile goal of making effective engineering interventions for cleaning up waterways and oceans)
children.textZhHtml:
根据我相当丰富的经验，非营利组织不仅充满了极其无能的人并由他们管理，而且还充满了傲慢和/或自欺欺人的人。我从来没有找到一个真正渴望寻求真理、效率等的组织。他们经常遵循他们的第一个（不可避免地不足）的想法，不仅拒绝所有批评，而且还以侮辱的方式回应，等等......

没有有意义/有能力的监督。这一切都与感觉和光学有关。因此，没有或不会取得真正的进展。

无论如何，是的，我同意有能力和真诚的人（极其罕见）应该努力对世界产生有意义的影响。但通常其他方面的钱更多。

（尽管我没有访问过他们，但我想到的一个罕见的例外是海洋清理项目。他们似乎正在尝试并成功实现了为清理水道和海洋进行有效的工程干预的有价值的目标）

children.author: swiftcoder
children.textRawHtml: To some extent, one doesn't even see the competent non-profits. They don't market aggressively, they don't scale up rapidly, they just stick to their niche and quietly hammer away at it for decades on end (often on things that have no feasible exit, like schools that will need to be externally funded forever)
children.textZhHtml: 在某种程度上，人们甚至看不到有能力的非营利组织。他们不会积极营销，也不会迅速扩大规模，他们只是坚持自己的利基市场，默默地持续数十年的努力（通常是在没有可行出口的事情上，比如需要外部资助的学校） 永远）

children.author: fragmede
children.textRawHtml: That seems like a simple money regulations issue. If the school has a $100 million endowment, but doesn't spend into it, the school would exist forever. If we say 10% returns a year, that's $10 million to spend on students in the form of teachers and classrooms and housing and books and everything. Unfortunately, that only teaches N students per year. But it would last ~forever. If, however, you get greedy with wanting to teach more than N students per year, then you get into the treadmill of needing external funding forever, but on the face of it, I don't accept that external funding is required forever.
children.textZhHtml: 这似乎是一个简单的货币监管问题。如果学校有 1 亿美元的捐赠，但不投入，学校就会永远存在。如果我们说每年 10% 的回报，那就是 1000 万美元以教师、教室、住房、书籍和一切形式花在学生身上。不幸的是，每年只教 N 个学生。但它会永远持续下去。然而，如果你因为想要每年教超过 N 个学生而变得贪婪，那么你就会陷入永远需要外部资金的困境，但从表面上看，我不接受永远需要外部资金。

children.author: swiftcoder
children.textRawHtml: A 100 million endowment is effectively "forever funding" from the perspective of a school that takes in maybe 200k/year of donations. You don't get 100 million endowments without the scale and marketing, unfortunately
children.textZhHtml: 从每年大约接受 20 万捐款的学校的角度来看，1 亿美元的捐赠实际上是“永久资助”。不幸的是，如果没有规模和营销，你就无法获得一亿的捐赠

children.author: idiotsecant
children.textRawHtml: There are plenty of nonprofits making a difference, your cynicism aside.
children.textZhHtml: 撇开你的愤世嫉俗不谈，有很多非营利组织正在发挥作用。

children.author: cwmoore
children.textRawHtml:
How is that difference measured, in general?

Setting aside cynicism is one thing but what answers are there for skepticism besides the very common moralizing personal attacks?

When I see a lot of nonprofit leadership improving their own lot much more reliably than the people “they serve”, I wonder if the handouts are just being politically diverted to the best and most politically valuable promoters.

If UBI is off the table, competition for gatekeeping resources becomes a dark market.
children.textZhHtml:

children.author: maeln
children.textRawHtml:
> It's one thing to build and ship 1000 bicycles to a poor village, but it's another to teach a village how to make bicycles

Buying and shipping X amount of Y to <country> is easy to calculate the cost. And it's a fixed one time cost. Perfect for PR OP, and humanitarian operation with limited budget and/or available work hours.

Teaching takes the most valuable resources of all: Time. And it's harder to predict how long (and therefor how much $$) it will take before having sustainable results. And it requires on-premise staff and usually to setup some building for the staff, for the teaching grounds, etc.

Tl;DR teaching can easily be 10X the time and money budget of a quick 'send stuff' operation. This is why these are usually big operation handled by big non-profit.
children.textZhHtml:
> 制造 1000 辆自行车并将其运送到一个贫困村庄是一回事，但教一个村庄如何制造自行车则是另一回事

购买 X 数量的 Y 并将其运送到 <国家> 的成本很容易计算。而且这是一项固定的一次性成本。非常适合预算和/或可用工作时间有限的公关行动和人道主义行动。

教学需要最宝贵的资源：时间。而且更难预测需要多长时间（以及需要多少美元）才能获得可持续的结果。它需要现场工作人员，通常需要为工作人员、教学场地等设置一些建筑物。

Tl;DR 教学可以轻松地将快速“发送内容”操作的时间和金钱预算增加 10 倍。这就是为什么这些通常是由大型非营利组织处理的大型行动。

children.author: ndr
children.textRawHtml:
There are game-theoretically aligned ways.

See for example https://oneacrefund.org/ [0] where they have a revolving loan fund.

OAF lends materials and teachers to farmers to make the more productive, by the end of the program they got productive enough to pay back the loan and OAF can lend the same money to someone else.

It's super capillary, with many boots on grounds and quality problems.

Embedded into this there's a good feedback channel: farmers who don't think are getting a good service stop paying back the loan. This allows OAF to go and audit what's failing there.

[0] The person who started also appeared in a podcast where they explained the basics https://foreveron.com/podcast/episode-035/
children.textZhHtml:
有一些博弈论一致的方法。

参见示例 https://oneacrefund.org/ [0] 他们有循环贷款基金。

OAF 向农民借出材料和教师，以提高农民的生产力，到项目结束时，他们的生产力足以偿还贷款，OAF 可以将同样的钱借给其他人。

这是超级毛细管，有很多靴子的理由和质量问题。

其中嵌入了一个良好的反馈渠道：认为没有得到良好服务的农民停止偿还贷款。这使得 OAF 能够去审核那里的失败之处。

[0] 发起人也出现在播客中，他们解释了基础知识 https://foreveron.com/podcast/episode-035/

children.author: fgdsgsdfdfgsdfg
children.textRawHtml:
This is of course all wonderful and the ideas presented here might also be useful for Western nations if the war machine starts gaining momentum in, say, a decade or two and things will turn uncomfortable.

That said, I'm also disgusted by the fact this is necessary at all. We designed and/or let an inherently unfair game go on unimpeded and give the losers some scraps so they may survive and continue to play along with can only be called the naturally occurring and less entertaining variant of The Hunger Games.

Any changes to the status quo will have to contend with powerful questions because why build bonds with people you distrust? Why bother including insignificant nations in your decision process? Why not be top dog and trample everyone under your righteous boots? Why not exploit and generally harvest the shit out of everything in sight and retrieve resources for the absolute minimum you can get away with? These sound annoying and they are, but they are really tough questions and they demand a good answer. Just "be a good person" is not cutting it. We need systemic solutions.

In case you're wondering I have the answer: sadly I do not, but I am convinced a couple of you do so please enlighten me.
children.textZhHtml:
这当然很棒，如果战争机器在一两年内开始增强动力并且事情会变得不舒服，那么这里提出的想法也可能对西方国家有用。

也就是说，我也对这是必要的事实感到厌恶。我们设计和/或让本质上不公平的游戏畅通无阻地进行，并给失败者一些碎片，这样他们就可以生存并继续玩下去，这只能被称为饥饿游戏的自然发生且缺乏娱乐性的变体。

对现状的任何改变都必须应对强有力的问题，因为为什么要与你不信任的人建立联系呢？为什么要把无关紧要的国家纳入你的决策过程呢？为什么不成为霸主，把所有人都踩在正义的靴子下呢？为什么不利用并从视野中的一切事物中普遍收获并以绝对最低的限度获取资源呢？这些听起来很烦人，确实如此，但它们确实是棘手的问题，需要一个好的答案。仅仅“做一个好人”并不能解决问题。我们需要系统性的解决方案。

如果您想知道我有答案：遗憾的是我没有，但我相信你们中的一些人会这样做，请启发我。

children.author: Jarwain
children.textRawHtml:
Imo it depends on priorities!

What do we care more about? The present? The current generation? Or future generations? Our children, grandchildren, great grandchildren? The perpetuation of human civilization? The perpetuation of human civilization with a set of values that that supports the growth and happiness of all?

That's the first question, right? Alignment. Sustainable alignment.

From there, it's all about sustaining those shared values, and minimizing risk. Build bonds: keep your friends close and your enemies closer. Or establish trust. It works out better in the long run; people don't usually Like to stab each other in the back Trampling starts cycles of revenge, and that's no fun for anyone involved either Exploitation prioritizes short term gain and screws you in the long run, more often than not.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为这取决于优先事项！

我们更关心什么？现在的？当前一代？还是后代？我们的孩子、孙子、曾孙？人类文明的延续？人类文明的延续是否需要一套支持所有人成长和幸福的价值观？

这是第一个问题，对吗？结盟。可持续的调整。

从那时起，一切都是为了维持这些共同的价值观，并最大限度地降低风险。 建立联系：与朋友保持密切联系，与敌人保持密切联系。或者建立信任。从长远来看，效果会更好；人们通常不喜欢互相背后捅刀子 践踏开始了复仇的循环，这对任何参与其中的人来说都没有乐趣 剥削通常会优先考虑短期利益，但从长远来看却会让你陷入困境。

children.author: Konnstann
children.textRawHtml:
https://www.buffalobicycle.com/storage/documents/Buffalo_com...

The buffalo bike is one that was specifically made for developing nations and the project prioritizes local assembly and repair, while the bike is designed to sacrifice weight and aerodynamics, instead offering heavier-duty parts like thicker rims, puncture-proof tires, high-capacity racks, etc. This bike has two chains to reduce the likelihood of a critical failure and an internal coaster brake hub which is more robust to the elements.

Your average low-cost bike isn't intended to be used in environments with rough terrain and high contaminant concentrations without regular maintenance, and especially older bikes with things like cup and cone bearings which are more susceptible to dirt getting in, thinner tires which puncture more easily, and nonstandard bits and pieces like derailleur hangers which predate the UDH standard.
children.textZhHtml:
https://www.buffalobicycle.com/storage/documents/Buffalo_com...

水牛自行车是一款专为发展中国家制造的自行车，该项目优先考虑本地组装和维修，而自行车的设计牺牲了重量和空气动力学性能，而是提供较重的部件，如更厚的轮圈、防刺穿轮胎、大容量行李架等。这款自行车有两条链条，可减少发生严重故障的可能性，还有一个内部过山车制动轮毂，更坚固耐用。

一般的低成本自行车不适合在地形崎岖和污染物浓度高且没有定期维护的环境中使用，尤其是那些较旧的自行车，其配备的轴承和锥体轴承更容易进灰尘，轮胎更薄，更容易刺穿，还有早于 UDH 标准的变速器吊架等非标准零件。

children.author: xmprt
children.textRawHtml:
Buffalo bicycles cost around $150 at least. That's frankly way too expensive to be a good bike for developing countries no matter how well suited it is for rough terrain.

The best bikes I saw were ones that a kid from a family could buy for maybe $20 in local currency and the repairability comes from the cheap cost - if something breaks down, you can find the replacement part from a spare bike that broke down months ago.
children.textZhHtml:
Buffalo 自行车至少要 150 美元左右。坦率地说，对于发展中国家来说，无论它多么适合崎岖的地形，对于发展中国家来说，这都太昂贵了。

我看到的最好的自行车是一个家庭的孩子可以用当地货币大约 20 美元购买的自行车，并且可维修性来自于低廉的成本 - 如果出现故障，您可以从几个月前坏掉的备用自行车中找到替换零件。

children.author: benj111
children.textRawHtml:
Well I'm not African so I don't know.

I'm thinking that in the west we either have very cheap bikes that aren't really designed for long term use, and more expensive bikes tend to use fancier parts.

Off the top of my head. Steel frame, can be repaired / modified with any old welder. Designed so it can be taken apart with the minimum of generic tools. Standard bearings, brake blocks etc (probably brake blocks that you can shove some piece of old tyre in).

Front forks and the crank require special tools to remove. I assume the free wheel assembly would be the same. I don't know if it would be possible to modify these to be serviceable with basic tools, the point is an African could probably work out how to fix a bike, the issue would be affording tools and spares, and availability of those tools and spares.
children.textZhHtml:
好吧，我不是非洲人，所以我不知道。

我认为在西方，我们要么拥有非常便宜的自行车，但并不是真正为长期使用而设计的，而更昂贵的自行车往往使用更高级的零件。

从我的头顶掉下来。钢框架，可以用任何旧焊机修理/改装。经过精心设计，可以使用最少的通用工具将其拆开。标准轴承、制动块等（可能是可以塞入一些旧轮胎的制动块）。

前叉和曲柄需要特殊工具才能拆卸。我认为自由轮组件是相同的。我不知道是否有可能修改这些以使用基本工具进行维修，关键是非洲人可能可以解决如何修理自行车，问题是提供工具和备件，以及这些工具和备件的可用性。

children.author: ErroneousBosh
children.textRawHtml:
Well you strip all the crap off, don't you? Just run the barest frame possible.

You still have the problem that the bits that you cannot just knock up out of old Morris Minor steering columns or AK47 barrels are the bits that break.

I know one person who has legitimately worn out a bike frame, in 40-something years of cycling, and it wasn't me. It was a guy who rode his bike about 50 miles *every single day* and rode from Edinburgh to Glasgow and back a couple of times a week. Eventually it started cracking around the welds.
children.textZhHtml:
好吧，你把所有的废话都脱掉了，不是吗？只需运行尽可能简单的框架即可。

您仍然会遇到这样的问题：您无法从旧的 Morris Minor 转向柱或 AK47 枪管中敲出的部件会损坏。

我认识一个人，在 40 多年的骑行生涯中，他的自行车车架确实磨损了，但那个人不是我。这个人*每天*骑自行车大约 50 英里，每周从爱丁堡骑到格拉斯哥，然后回来几次。最终，焊缝周围开始开裂。

children.author: nephihaha
children.textRawHtml: Have made a few suggestions above. Obviously I don't see Africa as a uniform continent, but extremes of dryness and humidity can be found in different parts, in some areas roads are bad and so on. Also good places for bike locks (which we all need), maybe water bottles, cargo features and lighting (since some roads will be unlit (again a general issue.)
children.textZhHtml: 上面已经提出了一些建议。显然，我不认为非洲是一个统一的大陆，但在不同的地方可以发现极端的干燥和潮湿，在某些地区道路很糟糕等等。也是放置自行车锁（我们都需要）、水瓶、货物设施和照明的好地方（因为有些道路将没有照明（这又是一个普遍问题）。

children.author: nephihaha
children.textRawHtml: Some parts of Africa are similar to elsewhere, but obvious things would be parts that can deal with either extreme dryness or humidity (both problems in different parts of the continent), good suspension (for areas with bad roads), bright colours (for visibility on shared roads), integration of bike locks and easy self-repair (for people in more remote areas).
children.textZhHtml: 非洲的某些地区与其他地区相似，但显而易见的是，部件可以应对极端干燥或潮湿（这两个问题在非洲大陆的不同地区都存在）、良好的悬架（适用于道路状况不佳的地区）、鲜艳的颜色（可提高共用道路上的可见度）、集成自行车锁和易于自我维修（适用于较偏远地区的人们）。

children.author: foldr
children.textRawHtml: Making your own bicycle is a waste of time and energy. If you’re buying in bulk from China, you can get a bike for around a $20 per unit cost. I know that $20 is a lot of money in some parts of the world, but it would make far more sense to organize bulk purchases than to try to start up some kind of ersatz low quality bike industry.
children.textZhHtml: 自己制作自行车是浪费时间和精力。如果您从中国批量购买自行车，单价约为 20 美元。我知道 20 美元在世界某些地方是一大笔钱，但组织批量采购比尝试创办某种仿造的低质量自行车行业更有意义。

children.author: internet_points
children.textRawHtml:
And Illich (where the term "tool" is quite broad, including anything from a knife to a highway system):

Tools foster conviviality to the extent to which they can be easily used, by anybody, as often or as seldom as desired, for the accomplishment of a purpose chosen by the user. The use of such tools by one person does not restrain another from using them equally. They do not require previous certification of the user. Their existence does not impose any obligation to use them. They allow the user to express his meaning in action.

Industrial tools deny this possibility to those who use them and they allow their designers to determine the meaning and expectations of others. Most tools today cannot be used in a convivial fashion.
children.textZhHtml:
还有 Illich（其中“工具”一词非常广泛，包括从刀到高速公路系统的任何东西）：

工具可以在一定程度上促进欢乐，任何人都可以根据需要经常或很少使用它们来实现用户选择的目的。一个人使用这些工具并不限制另一个人同样使用它们。它们不需要用户事先进行认证。它们的存在并不强加任何使用它们的义务。它们允许用户用行动表达他的意思。

工业工具否认了使用它们的人的这种可能性，并且它们允许设计者确定其他人的含义和期望。今天的大多数工具都不能以一种愉快的方式使用。

comments.author: CurtMonash
comments.textRawHtml: I recall predecessors to this idea from the 1970s, which probably implies I heard about them from Futurist Magazine and/or the book Small is Beautiful. It has always been suggested that engineers could do something worthwhile by inventing very simple yet useful things that could realistically be made in poor/underresourced countries or villages.

children.author: stdbrouw
children.textRawHtml: One aspect is perhaps that simple things aren't necessarily cheap. One Laptop per Child struggled to get costs down, whereas a mass market solar panel is not self-serviceable but at their current cost, who cares. Even in the developed world, you get noticeably more bang for your buck if you buy a cheap, basic car than if you buy a cargo bike that could replace it, because of the fierce competition and huge economies of scale for cars. Touch screens are cheaper than seven segment displays. And so on. (Not a snub on the Open Source Low Tech community, their designs look great!)
children.textZhHtml: One aspect is perhaps that simple things aren't necessarily cheap.每个孩子一台笔记本电脑都在努力降低成本，而大众市场的太阳能电池板不是自助式的，但按照目前的成本，谁在乎呢。即使在发达国家，由于汽车的激烈竞争和巨大的规模经济，如果你购买一辆便宜的基本型汽车，你会比购买一辆可以替代它的货运自行车更划算。 Touch screens are cheaper than seven segment displays.等等。 (Not a snub on the Open Source Low Tech community, their designs look great!)

children.author: nchmy
children.textRawHtml:
Yeah, I agree with this. Though, I think you could make bikes for much cheaper with some ingenuity. Certainly some bike-powered appliances.

Also I think OLPC was a misguided project, as most of these things are. Its out of stem with maslows hierarchy of needs. Folks who are subsistence farmers with drought, smoke filled homes and stomach parasites don't need a laptop and academic education. They need practical and actionable tools and techniques to better meet their basic needs, a state from which they could better pursue academics etc
children.textZhHtml:

我还认为 OLPC 是一个被误导的项目，就像大多数项目一样。这与马斯洛的需求层次不符。那些饱受干旱、烟雾缭绕、胃部寄生虫困扰的自给农民不需要笔记本电脑和学术教育。他们需要实用且可操作的工具和技术来更好地满足他们的基本需求，这是他们可以更好地追求学术等的状态

children.author: sublinear
children.textRawHtml:
As other comments have implied, but not explicitly said, they do their own engineering already with what they have.

Since materials can be scarce and inconsistent, much of it is improvised. That in no way diminishes their efforts, results, or knowledge. If anything, that's way more impressive. Lots of engineers in the first world will throw a tantrum if they can't have things exactly their way and probably still make something that doesn't work as well. Entire businesses have shit their pants and gone bankrupt the moment a part is discontinued.

Trying to do better than the people who live there is not only arrogant, but it's own variation of Chesterton's Fence.

EDIT: I can't help myself and have to post an engineerguy video. It's too important of a topic to not drive this point home crystal clear by someone who is far more respected than me.

"Building a Cathedral without Science or Mathematics: The Engineering Method Explained"

https://youtube.com/watch?v=_ivqWN4L3zU
children.textZhHtml:
正如其他评论暗示但没有明确指出的那样， 他们自己做工程 已经有了他们所拥有的。

由于材料可能稀缺且不一致，因此大部分都是临时制作的。这绝不会削弱他们的努力、成果或知识。如果有的话，那就更令人印象深刻了。第一世界的许多工程师如果不能完全按照自己的方式做事，并且可能仍然制造出效果不佳的东西，他们就会发脾气。一旦某个零件停产，整个企业就会尿裤子并破产。

试图比住在那里的人做得更好不仅是傲慢，而且是切斯特顿栅栏的变种。

编辑：我无法控制自己，不得不发布一个工程师视频。这个话题太重要了，以至于比我更受尊敬的人都清楚地阐明了这一点。

“建造一座没有科学或数学的大教堂：工程方法解释”

https://youtube.com/watch?v=_ivqWN4L3zU

children.author: samuell
children.textRawHtml:
As someone who has spent some time in sub-saharan Africa, I can tell you that there is lots that can be done if just seeing the possibilities.

Not everyone everywhere are "engineering oriented", and having people with the skills and eye for practical solutions based on available materials, can help tons, and also open up people's imagination for what can be done.

In fact, this goes for northern Europe too, just that more people can manage without home-built solutions and can "buy away the problem" here.

Also, people where immensely thankful e.g. when my quite clever and crafty father managed to repair a water tank tower that'd been broken for months and years, by sourcing some local material, coming up with a repair design, and having local welders create it, etc etc.
children.textZhHtml:
作为一个在撒哈拉以南非洲呆过一段时间的人，我可以告诉你，只要看到可能性，就有很多事情可以做。

并不是每个地方的每个人都“以工程为导向”，拥有具有技能和眼光的人员，能够根据现有材料找到实用的解决方案，可以帮助很多人，也可以开拓人们对可以做什么的想象力。

事实上，这也适用于北欧，只是更多的人可以在没有自制解决方案的情况下进行管理，并且可以在这里“解决问题”。

此外，人们非常感激，例如当我非常聪明和狡猾的父亲设法修复了一个已经损坏了数月和数年的水箱塔时，他通过采购一些当地材料，提出修复设计，并让当地焊工进行制作等等。

children.author: sublinear
children.textRawHtml:
I've never been to sub-saharan Africa, but I did grow up broke enough to be on the receiving end of people with good intentions.

I'm not saying you're necessarily wrong, but the realities of a situation guided by those who more deeply understand it can very quickly prune the possibilities that an engineer sees. Many engineers would just get frustrated and give up. Many leaders would become impatient with those engineers.

I think to get people's heads out of the clouds and produce real results requires a very special kind of engineer. That is most likely going to be someone local, not an outsider. One can definitely help on the education side of things for the locals, but I'm not convinced that's where the real problems are. It's more likely political and economic. Not even the best engineers in the world are going to solve that.
children.textZhHtml:
我从未去过撒哈拉以南非洲，但我确实是在穷困潦倒的成长过程中成为好心人的接收者的。

我并不是说你一定是错的，但在那些更深入了解情况的人的指导下，现实情况可以很快消除工程师看到的可能性。许多工程师会感到沮丧并放弃。许多领导者会对这些工程师感到不耐烦。

我认为，要让人们摆脱云计算并产生真正的结果，需要一种非常特殊的工程师。那很可能是当地人，而不是外地人。人们绝对可以在当地人的教育方面提供帮助，但我不相信这就是真正的问题所在。更有可能是政治和经济原因。即使是世界上最好的工程师也无法解决这个问题。

children.author: nchmy
children.textRawHtml:
I suspect you've never spent any meaningful amount of time in the local environments, or you wouldn't have made this comment.

As I elaborated on in another comment, it is commonplace to find that people who have little to no education and resources are missing countless opportunities to implement simple improvements to almost everything.

This mindset of "locals know best" is, frankly, toxic. (just think about the locals wherever you live to see how incompetent they are as well)

What is needed is genuine collaboration and communication between people living in whatever situation and others who are fortunate to have more access to information, resources, education on critical thinking etc...
children.textZhHtml:
我怀疑您从未在当地环境中度过过有意义的时间，否则您不会发表此评论。

children.author: AnthonyMouse
children.textRawHtml:
The trouble with your premise is that it doesn't just apply to local people, it applies to people in general. Incompetence is all around us. Notice that everyone is a local in the place where they live.

But local people at least have the benefit of knowing their own situations, which puts them ahead of some other rando who doesn't even have that and presumes to know better.
children.textZhHtml:
你的前提的问题在于它不仅适用于当地人，也适用于一般人。无能无处不在。请注意，每个人都是他们居住地的当地人。

但当地人至少有一个好处，那就是了解自己的情况，这使他们领先于其他一些甚至不了解自己情况却自以为了解得更多的人。

children.author: nchmy
children.textRawHtml: And hence, AS I SAID, people should collaboratively work together. There's no presumption - despite you recognizing that incompetence is all around us, you'd be amazed at how many completely obvious things are not done, and other things done very poorly
children.textZhHtml: 因此，正如我所说，人们应该协同工作。没有任何假设——尽管你认识到我们周围到处都是无能，但你会惊讶地发现有多少完全显而易见的事情没有做，而其他事情做得很糟糕

children.author: AnthonyMouse
children.textRawHtml:
Which is a misunderstanding of the premise that locals know best.

No one thinks that going to someone and offering them ideas is bad. The thing people actually object to is imperialism, and they object to it because it's common. All over the place you see not just governments making rules from an ivory tower, but even well-meaning non-profits providing grants or resources with the requirement that things be done in a particular way with no regard for local conditions.

If you're trying to help someone then you shouldn't need to coerce them. Nobody was ever objecting to the cases where you don't.
children.textZhHtml:
这是对当地人最了解的前提的误解。

没有人认为去找某人并向他们提供 想法 很糟糕。人们实际上反对的是帝国主义，他们反对它是因为它是普遍的。在所有地方，你不仅看到政府在象牙塔里制定规则，甚至善意的非营利组织也提供赠款或资源，要求以特定方式做事，而不考虑当地条件。

如果你想帮助某人，那么你不需要强迫他们。没有人会反对你不反对的情况。

children.author: nchmy
children.textRawHtml:
Actually, plenty of people - including the person I was replying to - explicitly argue that locals know best and you're being imperialist by even suggesting that outsiders might have anything useful to contribute other than money.

And, yes, you do need to "coerce"/convince/persuade/retrain people across many dimensions - people are generally extremely resistant to change, and instead stick with whatever theyre already doing. Not all interventions are just immediately self-evidently beneficial. Eg. "Hey stop destroying your topsoil by every means imaginable - removing and burning all waste from the harvest, not planting in contours to prevent erosion" and much much more. That takes time to prove out and even then requires a leap of faith.

I do strongly agree, though, that "well meaning" non-profits are quite often completely clueless and, arguably, destructive. Often through the very gifts they give, which often don't even work, or at least go counter towards the necessary goal of developing self-sufficiency.
children.textZhHtml:
事实上，很多人——包括我回复的人——明确认为当地人最了解，你甚至暗示外人除了金钱之外可能有任何有用的东西可以贡献，这是帝国主义。

而且，是的，你确实需要在多个方面“强制”/说服/说服/再培训人们——人们通常非常抵制改变，而是坚持他们已经在做的事情。并非所有干预措施都会立即产生不言而喻的益处。例如。 “嘿，停止以各种可以想象的方式破坏你的表土——清除和燃烧收获中的所有废物，而不是按轮廓种植以防止侵蚀”等等。这需要时间来证明，即便如此，也需要信念的飞跃。

不过，我非常同意，“善意”的非营利组织往往完全毫无头绪，而且可以说是具有破坏性的。通常，他们所给予的礼物往往甚至不起作用，或者至少与发展自给自足的必要目标背道而驰。

children.author: contingencies
children.textRawHtml:
Yes, an air conditioner can be made as follows.

1. Identify a locally viable deciduous tree species.

2. Plant this species on the afternoon aspect of the sun in your location. So for northern hemisphere plant to the southwest. For southern hemisphere plant to the northwest.

3. Enjoy shade in summer and sun in winter, plus cleaner air.

Another option depending upon your architecture is to take advantage of forced air within a roof cavity. Buy a large diameter fan with an electronic control, install at apex, and set it to operate when the temperature exceeds a certain point. This will result in a house wide temperature drop with no need to expend $$$ setup plus dollars per hour per room on air conditioning.

Another option is simply shading. One of the simplest and most traditional approaches here is the drop awning. A drop awning consists of a retractable material expanse which can render a building surface in shade and thus promote rapid air movement and cooling in the hottest periods.

Two final options include internal forced air: passive variant (open all the windows and doors) or active variant (ceiling fan, solar works) and insulation or thermal mass.
children.textZhHtml:
是的，空调可以按如下方式制作。

1. 确定当地可行的落叶树种。

2. 在您所在地的午后阳光下种植该物种。因此对于北半球来说，植物位于西南方。为南半球植物向西北。

3. 夏季享受阴凉，冬季享受阳光，空气清新。

根据您的架构，另一种选择是利用屋顶空腔内的强制空气。购买一个带电子控制的大直径风扇，安装在顶点，并设置为当温度超过某一点时运行。这将导致整个房屋的温度下降，而无需花费 $$$ 设置以及每个房间每小时的空调费用。

另一种选择是简单的阴影。这里最简单和最传统的方法之一是下降遮阳篷。垂落遮阳篷由可伸缩的材料组成，可以使建筑物表面处于阴凉处，从而在最热的时期促进空气的快速流动和冷却。

最后两个选项包括内部强制通风：被动变体（打开所有门窗）或主动变体（吊扇、太阳能装置）以及隔热或热质量。

children.textRawHtml: I would argue that, for anything with value, there is value in having a decent open source option for that thing. I think farm equipment is valuable, so I think it's great that someone is working on an open source option. You are right though, it is an enormously uphill battle. To get off the ground, there would likely need to be a well funded non profit involved. Would be cool to see Futo or similar tackle something like this
children.textZhHtml: 我认为，对于任何有价值的东西，拥有一个像样的开源选项是有价值的。我认为农场设备很有价值，所以我认为有人正在研究开源选项是件好事。不过，你是对的，这是一场极其艰苦的战斗。为了起步，可能需要有一个资金充足的非营利组织参与。看到 Futo 或类似的解决类似问题会很酷

children.author: hobofan
children.textRawHtml:
I think it makes more sense if it's not viewed under the aspect of trying to be maximally productive in its output. If you look at it over they years, they've also tried to do a share of public outreach / education (though I'm not sure how successful), and I think they also sustain themselves in terms of labor with burnt out tech people that are looking for a change of scenery.

So similar to a good zoo, that does both active conservation work, and at the same time public education (e.g. in the form of guided tours for school trips).

> I guess that's why it seems to languish in obscurity.

I think even in a well-executed form, it would likely still be quite obscure, as there is next to no need for it in western societies (apart from emergency preperation).
children.textZhHtml:
我认为，如果不从试图最大限度地提高产出的角度来看待它，那就更有意义了。如果你看看他们多年来的情况，他们还尝试做一些公共宣传/教育（尽管我不确定有多成功），而且我认为他们也在劳动力方面与那些正在寻求改变环境的精疲力竭的技术人员一起维持自己。

与一个好的动物园非常相似，它既进行积极的保护工作，同时又进行公共教育（例如以学校旅行的导游形式）。

> 我想这就是为什么它似乎在默默无闻中衰弱的原因。

我认为即使以良好执行的形式，它可能仍然相当模糊，因为在西方社会几乎不需要它（除了紧急准备之外）。
